description: One-time repo-wide flip: turn on TypeScript strict mode. Decision (one-time choice), not Convention (forward-looking rule).

conversation.title: Turning on TypeScript strict mode in the monorepo

turns.text: Half the packages in the monorepo run with `strict: false` and the other half are strict. I keep getting bitten by type-narrowing bugs in the loose ones. Should we just bite the bullet and turn strict on everywhere?

Yes, but stage it. Flip `strict: true` at the root `tsconfig.base.json` so new code defaults to strict, then add `// @ts-nocheck` or a per-package override only on the packages that don't yet pass. That gives you a finite, visible backlog (the count of overrides) instead of a moving 'one day we'll be strict' target. Doing it as a single big-bang diff is technically simpler but reviewers can't usefully look at a 4000-line PR; staging keeps each package's diff reviewable.

Going with that. Decision: `tsconfig.base.json` flips to `strict: true` this sprint. Each package that doesn't pass adds a per-package override `tsconfig.json` with `strict: false`, treated as a tracked migration backlog (one ticket per package, owner = whoever last touched it). No new packages may add a `strict: false` override after this sprint — that's the gate.

Solid. Two implementation notes: (1) `noImplicitAny` and `strictNullChecks` produce 90% of the diff churn — if you want to stage *within* a package, those two are the natural sub-flags to flip first. (2) `tsc --noEmit` in CI on each package gives you the per-package override-status report for free.

must_not_contain: new code MUST always pass strict
expected.note: Flip strict mode on at the root tsconfig — a one-time repo state change. NOT a forward-looking rule (which would be a convention); the gate clause about future packages is part of the impact, not a separate convention.
